Finally, driver_age shows a pronounced long-term decline, dropping from a mean near 38–39 years in the early 1950s to approximately 27–28 years by the 2010s before a slight recent uptick. This reflects the professionalization of Formula One and the increasing entry of younger drivers, particularly from feeder series, in the modern era. Taken together, these drift patterns confirm that the dataset exhibits significant temporal non-stationarity, and that era-based features and time-aware train–test splits are essential for building models that generalize well to recent seasons.

Correlation Analysis¶

In [46]:
# Numeric feature set for correlation (exclude IDs + targets)
corr_features = [c for c in numeric_cols if c not in id_cols + target_cols]

# Core numeric variables to include for context
# IMPORTANT: positionOrder, points, milliseconds, and laps are POST-RACE outcomes
# They CANNOT be used as model features — they directly encode or correlate with the target.
# Only grid (starting position) is a legitimate pre-race feature.
core_numeric = ["grid"]
core_numeric = [c for c in core_numeric if c in df.columns]

# Combine engineered + core numeric features (unique, ordered)
corr_pick = list(dict.fromkeys(engineered_features + core_numeric))
corr_pick = [
    c for c in corr_pick
    if c in df.columns and pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df[c])
]

# Compute correlation matrix
corr_mat = df[corr_pick].corr()

# Heatmap
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(16, 12))

mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr_mat, dtype=bool))

sns.heatmap(
    corr_mat,
    mask=mask,
    annot=False,
    cmap="Purples",
    center=0,
    square=True,
    linewidths=0.5,
    cbar_kws={"label": "Correlation"},
    ax=ax
)

ax.set_title(
    "Correlation Heatmap of Engineered and Core Numeric Features",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold",
    color=NU_COLORS["purple"]
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close(fig)

The correlation heatmap reveals several meaningful inter-feature relationships across the engineered feature set while also confirming that most features remain sufficiently distinct to contribute independent information to the model. The strongest correlations appear within logically related feature clusters. Grid position features show high internal correlation, as expected: grid_position_numeric, grid, grid_percentile, max_position_gain, front_row_start, top5_start, and top10_start all move together, since they are all derived from the same underlying starting position signal. Similarly, the driver performance features — driver_prev_5_top10_rate, driver_career_top10_rate, driver_constructor_top10_rate, and driver_circuit_top10_rate — are moderately correlated with one another, reflecting that consistent drivers tend to score well across all performance horizons. The championship context features championship_points, in_championship_contention, and points_gap_to_leader form another correlated cluster, which is expected since they all capture season-standing position from different angles. driver_prev_5_top10_rate and driver_prev_5_dnf_rate show a notable negative relationship, confirming that drivers finishing more frequently in the top 10 are also less likely to retire from races. Pit stop features (driver_avg_pit_stops, constructor_avg_pit_time) and circuit characteristics (circuit_avg_dnf_rate, circuit_position_volatility) exhibit relatively low correlations with most other features, suggesting they contribute genuinely distinct contextual signals. Overall, the heatmap confirms that while some redundancy exists within feature groups — particularly among grid-derived and performance-rate features — the engineered feature set as a whole captures multiple non-overlapping dimensions of pre-race information, which is desirable for building robust predictive models.

Top Correlated Feature Pairs¶

In [47]:
# Extract strongest correlations (absolute value, upper triangle only)
corr_pairs = (
    corr_mat.abs()
    .where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_mat.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
    .stack()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .reset_index()
)

corr_pairs.columns = ["Feature 1", "Feature 2", "Absolute Correlation"]

# Display top 20 correlations
nu_style_table(
    corr_pairs.head(20),
    caption="Top 20 Absolute Correlations Among Engineered Features"
)
Out[47]:
Top 20 Absolute Correlations Among Engineered Features
  Feature 1 Feature 2 Absolute Correlation
0 grid_position_numeric grid 1.000000
1 max_position_gain grid 1.000000
2 grid_position_numeric max_position_gain 1.000000
3 grid_percentile grid 0.953784
4 grid_position_numeric grid_percentile 0.953784
5 grid_percentile max_position_gain 0.953784
6 driver_prev_5_top10_rate driver_prev_5_avg_position 0.853163
7 top10_start grid 0.850827
8 grid_position_numeric top10_start 0.850827
9 top10_start max_position_gain 0.850827
10 grid_percentile top10_start 0.847193
11 constructor_prev_5_top10_rate constructor_season_top10_rate 0.810297
12 driver_prev_5_top10_rate driver_constructor_top10_rate 0.777559
13 driver_points_last3 championship_points 0.759860
14 driver_career_points_per_race driver_points_last3 0.758429
15 driver_career_top10_rate driver_constructor_top10_rate 0.756741
16 grid_percentile top5_start 0.751283
17 grid_position_numeric top5_start 0.748568
18 top5_start grid 0.748568
19 top5_start max_position_gain 0.748568

The top 20 correlated feature pairs table quantifies the strongest linear relationships identified visually in the heatmap and highlights two distinct sources of redundancy in the feature set. The first is within the grid position feature group, where grid_position_numeric, max_position_gain, grid, and grid_percentile are all perfectly or near-perfectly correlated with one another (ρ = 1.00 and 0.95), which is expected since max_position_gain is defined directly as a linear transformation of grid position and grid_percentile is a monotonic rescaling of the same variable. The binary indicators top10_start, top5_start, and front_row_start also correlate strongly with this group (ρ ranging from 0.75 to 0.85), as they are threshold-based encodings of the same underlying signal. In practice, including all of these features simultaneously in a linear model would introduce multicollinearity, though tree-based models are generally robust to this. The second source of redundancy appears among the rolling and expanding performance rate features: driver_prev_5_top10_rate correlates strongly with both driver_prev_5_avg_position (ρ ≈ 0.85) and driver_constructor_top10_rate (ρ ≈ 0.78), and constructor_prev_5_top10_rate correlates with constructor_season_top10_rate (ρ ≈ 0.81), reflecting that short-term and longer-term performance trends tend to align for consistently strong or weak drivers and teams. The championship context features driver_points_last3 and championship_points are also notably correlated (ρ ≈ 0.76), since recent points accumulation naturally tracks overall season standing. Despite these correlations, retaining the full feature set is defensible for tree-based models, while feature selection or dimensionality reduction may be considered for linear approaches to mitigate multicollinearity concerns.

Leakage Check: Pre-Race Features Only¶

Before training any models, we remove columns that would only be known after the race (for example finishing position, points, race time, and in-race pit stop totals).
Keeping only pre-race information prevents data leakage and makes our validation and test results realistic.

In [48]:
# 1) Collect candidate feature groups
encoded_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.endswith("_encoded")]     # label-encoded categoricals
season_ohe_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("season_")] # one-hot season stage
base_feature_cols = list(dict.fromkeys(corr_pick + encoded_cols + season_ohe_cols))  # unique, keep order

# 2) Explicitly block POST-RACE / outcome-leaking columns
#    These fields are known only after the race (or directly define the target),
#    so they must never be used as predictors.
leakage_cols = {
    "positionOrder", "position", "rank",          # finishing outcome (defines target)
    "points",                                   # points depend on finishing position
    "milliseconds", "laps",                      # race-time completion metrics (post-race)
    "fastestLap", "fastestLapTime", "fastestLapSpeed",  # post-race stats
    "statusId",                                 # finishing / DNF signal
    "num_pit_stops", "total_pit_time_ms", "avg_pit_time_ms"  # actual in-race pit behavior
}

# 3) Remove targets + leakage + any columns that do not exist
target_cols_full = {"top10_finish", "points_finish", "podium_finish", "dnf"}

feature_cols = [
    c for c in base_feature_cols
    if (c in df.columns) and (c not in target_cols_full) and (c not in leakage_cols)
]

# 4) Final model matrix
X = df[feature_cols].copy()
y = df["top10_finish"].astype(int)

# 5) Quick summary (clean, readable)
feature_matrix_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Item": ["Observations", "Features (pre-race only)", "Removed leakage columns", "Removed target columns"],
    "Value": [f"{X.shape[0]:,}", f"{X.shape[1]:,}", f"{len(leakage_cols):,}", f"{len(target_cols_full):,}"]
})

display(nu_style_table(feature_matrix_summary, caption="Leakage-Safe Feature Matrix Summary"))

# Optional: show the final feature list (first 30 to keep output small)
feature_list_preview = pd.DataFrame({"Feature": feature_cols[:30]})
display(nu_style_table(feature_list_preview, caption="Feature List Preview (First 30)"))
print(f"Full feature list length: {len(feature_cols)}")
Leakage-Safe Feature Matrix Summary
  Item Value
0 Observations 26,759
1 Features (pre-race only) 45
2 Removed leakage columns 13
3 Removed target columns 4
Feature List Preview (First 30)
  Feature
0 driver_age
1 driver_prev_5_top10_rate
2 driver_prev_5_dnf_rate
3 driver_prev_5_avg_position
4 driver_career_races
5 driver_career_top10_rate
6 driver_career_points_per_race
7 constructor_prev_5_top10_rate
8 constructor_prev_5_dnf_rate
9 constructor_season_top10_rate
10 circuit_avg_dnf_rate
11 circuit_position_volatility
12 driver_circuit_top10_rate
13 driver_constructor_races
14 driver_constructor_top10_rate
15 grid_position_numeric
16 grid_percentile
17 front_row_start
18 top5_start
19 top10_start
20 max_position_gain
21 num_competitors
22 driver_top10_streak
23 driver_points_last3
24 driver_position_trend
25 driver_avg_pit_stops
26 constructor_avg_pit_time
27 championship_points
28 championship_position
29 in_championship_contention 
Full feature list length: 45

This step constructs the final leakage-safe feature matrix used for all downstream modeling. A total of 13 post-race columns are explicitly blocked before assembling the feature set, including finishing position, race points, lap counts, race timing statistics, fastest lap metrics, pit stop actuals, and DNF status — all of which are only observable after the race concludes and would therefore constitute data leakage if included as predictors. The four target-related outcome variables are also excluded separately. After applying these filters, the final feature matrix retains all 26,759 observations and 45 pre-race features, combining the 33 engineered performance and context features with label-encoded categorical variables and one-hot encoded season stage indicators. The feature list preview confirms that the first 30 features span all major engineered feature groups: driver form and career history, constructor performance, circuit characteristics, grid position signals, momentum indicators, pit stop proxies, and championship context. This breadth ensures that the model has access to a rich and diverse set of pre-race signals while remaining strictly realistic — every feature in the matrix represents information that would genuinely be available to a forecaster before the race starts, making the subsequent validation and test results a reliable reflection of true out-of-sample predictive performance.

Temporal Train / Validation / Test Split¶

In [49]:
# Temporal three-way split
TRAIN_END = 2018      # Train: < 2018
VAL_END = 2022        # Validation: 2018–2021 | Test: 2022+

train_idx = df["year"] < TRAIN_END
val_idx = (df["year"] >= TRAIN_END) & (df["year"] < VAL_END)
test_idx = df["year"] >= VAL_END

X_train, y_train = X.loc[train_idx], y.loc[train_idx]
X_val, y_val     = X.loc[val_idx],   y.loc[val_idx]
X_test, y_test   = X.loc[test_idx],  y.loc[test_idx]

# Create summary table
split_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Split": [
        f"Train (< {TRAIN_END})",
        f"Validation ({TRAIN_END}–{VAL_END-1})",
        f"Test (≥ {VAL_END})",
        "Total"
    ],
    "Rows": [
        len(X_train),
        len(X_val),
        len(X_test),
        len(X)
    ],
    "Top-10 Rate": [
        f"{y_train.mean():.3f}",
        f"{y_val.mean():.3f}",
        f"{y_test.mean():.3f}",
        f"{y.mean():.3f}"
    ]
})

nu_style_table(
    split_summary,
    caption="Temporal Train, Validation, and Test Split Summary"
)
Out[49]:
Temporal Train, Validation, and Test Split Summary
  Split Rows Top-10 Rate
0 Train (< 2018) 23780 0.413
1 Validation (2018–2021) 1620 0.500
2 Test (≥ 2022) 1359 0.500
3 Total 26759 0.423

The dataset is divided into three temporally ordered splits to reflect a realistic forecasting setting where models are trained on historical seasons and evaluated on future, unseen ones. The training set covers all races before 2018 and contains 23,780 observations, representing approximately 89% of the full dataset and spanning the majority of Formula One history from 1950 onward. The validation set covers the 2018–2021 seasons with 1,620 observations, and the test set covers seasons from 2022 onward with 1,359 observations. A key observation is the notable difference in top-10 finish rates across splits: the training set has a rate of 41.3%, while both the validation and test sets sit at 50.0%, meaningfully higher than the training baseline. This gap reflects the structural shift in modern Formula One toward smaller, more standardized grids where a larger proportion of entries finish in the top 10, and it reinforces the importance of the era-based features and temporal modeling choices made earlier. Importantly, this temporal split design ensures there is no overlap between training and evaluation periods, preventing any form of future data leakage into model training. The relatively small size of the validation and test sets is a direct consequence of limiting evaluation to recent seasons, which is the appropriate trade-off for realistic assessment of how models would perform when deployed for upcoming races.

Scaling (fit on TRAIN only to avoid leakage)¶

In [50]:
scaler = StandardScaler()

X_train_scaled = pd.DataFrame(
    scaler.fit_transform(X_train),
    columns=X_train.columns,
    index=X_train.index
)

X_val_scaled = pd.DataFrame(
    scaler.transform(X_val),
    columns=X_val.columns,
    index=X_val.index
)

X_test_scaled = pd.DataFrame(
    scaler.transform(X_test),
    columns=X_test.columns,
    index=X_test.index
)

print('Scaling complete (train-fit only).')
print('Scaled shapes:', X_train_scaled.shape, X_val_scaled.shape, X_test_scaled.shape)

Scaling complete (train-fit only).
Scaled shapes: (23780, 45) (1620, 45) (1359, 45)

Feature scaling is applied using a StandardScaler that is fitted exclusively on the training set and then used to transform the validation and test sets using the same learned mean and standard deviation values. This approach is a critical safeguard against data leakage: fitting the scaler on the full dataset before splitting would allow distributional information from future seasons to influence the scaling of training features, subtly inflating model performance estimates. By fitting only on training data, the scaling transformation reflects only what would realistically be known at training time. The output confirms that all three splits retain the full 45-feature matrix with shapes of 23,780, 1,620, and 1,359 rows respectively, consistent with the temporal split defined in the previous step. Scaled versions are used specifically for models that are sensitive to feature magnitude — Logistic Regression and MLP — while tree-based models such as Random Forest and Gradient Boosting are trained on the unscaled feature matrix, since they are invariant to monotonic transformations of input features.

LASSO Feature Importance (Linear Baseline)¶

In [51]:
# LASSO (train-only, leakage-safe)
lasso = LogisticRegression(
    penalty='l1',
    solver='saga',
    max_iter=5000,
    random_state=42,
    class_weight='balanced'
)

lasso.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

lasso_importance = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': X_train.columns,
    'Coefficient': lasso.coef_[0]
})

lasso_importance['Abs Coefficient'] = lasso_importance['Coefficient'].abs()
lasso_importance = lasso_importance.sort_values('Abs Coefficient', ascending=False)
lasso_nonzero = lasso_importance[lasso_importance['Coefficient'] != 0].reset_index(drop=True)

display(
    nu_style_table(
        lasso_nonzero.head(20),
        caption='Top Features Selected by LASSO (Train-Only, Leakage-Safe)'
    )
)
Top Features Selected by LASSO (Train-Only, Leakage-Safe)
  Feature Coefficient Abs Coefficient
0 driver_prev_5_avg_position -0.401268 0.401268
1 front_row_start -0.319673 0.319673
2 constructor_season_top10_rate 0.249743 0.249743
3 constructor_prev_5_top10_rate 0.168447 0.168447
4 driver_prev_5_dnf_rate 0.142657 0.142657
5 circuit_position_volatility -0.138563 0.138563
6 driver_points_last3 0.136702 0.136702
7 grid_percentile -0.133443 0.133443
8 circuit_avg_top10_rate -0.132562 0.132562
9 championship_points 0.115550 0.115550
10 top5_start -0.108749 0.108749
11 grid_position_numeric -0.085827 0.085827
12 grid -0.085827 0.085827
13 max_position_gain -0.085827 0.085827
14 driver_constructor_races 0.083186 0.083186
15 num_competitors -0.083053 0.083053
16 points_gap_to_leader -0.076766 0.076766
17 driver_career_points_per_race 0.064633 0.064633
18 driver_prev_5_top10_rate -0.060604 0.060604
19 driver_career_races 0.056405 0.056405

The LASSO logistic regression applies L1 regularization to shrink coefficients of weak or redundant features to exactly zero, effectively performing automatic feature selection while fitting a linear model. Fitted on the scaled training data only, the model retains 20 features with non-zero coefficients out of the full 45, providing a compact linear view of which pre-race signals carry the most independent predictive value. The strongest single predictor is driver_prev_5_avg_position with a negative coefficient of −0.40, indicating that lower (better) recent average finishing positions strongly increase the probability of a top-10 finish, as expected. front_row_start carries the second largest absolute coefficient at −0.32, confirming the well-established advantage of starting near the front of the grid. On the constructor side, constructor_season_top10_rate and constructor_prev_5_top10_rate both receive positive coefficients, reflecting that drivers in stronger teams are more likely to finish in the top 10. Notably, driver_prev_5_dnf_rate receives a positive coefficient, which may appear counterintuitive but likely reflects a historical era effect where high DNF rates were universal and surviving to finish was itself a top-10 predictor. circuit_position_volatility and circuit_avg_top10_rate are both selected with meaningful negative coefficients, capturing the role of track-specific difficulty. The championship context features championship_points and points_gap_to_leader are also retained, suggesting that season standing carries incremental linear signal beyond recent form alone. The presence of correlated grid features — grid_position_numeric, grid, and max_position_gain — all receiving identical coefficients reflects their perfect multicollinearity, and in practice one representative from this group would suffice for a linear model.

Tree-Based Feature Importance (Non-Linear)¶

In [52]:
# Random Forest importance (train-only, leakage-safe)
rf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=300,
    max_depth=10,
    random_state=42,
    n_jobs=-1
)

rf.fit(X_train, y_train)

rf_importance = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': feature_cols,
    'Importance': rf.feature_importances_
}).sort_values('Importance', ascending=False)

# Keep rf_imp for dashboard compatibility
rf_imp = pd.Series(rf.feature_importances_, index=feature_cols).sort_values(ascending=False)

display(
    nu_style_table(
        rf_importance.head(20),
        caption='Top Features by Random Forest Importance (Train-Only, Leakage-Safe)'
    )
)
Top Features by Random Forest Importance (Train-Only, Leakage-Safe)
  Feature Importance
9 constructor_season_top10_rate 0.075750
33 grid 0.065099
28 championship_position 0.062907
15 grid_position_numeric 0.062034
20 max_position_gain 0.060979
23 driver_points_last3 0.057254
3 driver_prev_5_avg_position 0.055048
14 driver_constructor_top10_rate 0.053289
27 championship_points 0.036625
16 grid_percentile 0.036310
5 driver_career_top10_rate 0.034763
6 driver_career_points_per_race 0.031418
1 driver_prev_5_top10_rate 0.031262
7 constructor_prev_5_top10_rate 0.029071
11 circuit_position_volatility 0.020600
10 circuit_avg_dnf_rate 0.020584
31 circuit_avg_top10_rate 0.018214
4 driver_career_races 0.017784
29 in_championship_contention 0.017418
30 points_gap_to_leader 0.016251

The Random Forest feature importance scores, derived from mean impurity decrease across 300 trees trained on the unscaled training data, reveal a broader and more distributed picture of predictive signal compared to the sparse LASSO selection. No single feature dominates heavily, with the top feature constructor_season_top10_rate accounting for approximately 7.6% of total importance, followed closely by grid and championship_position at around 6.5% and 6.3% respectively. This relatively flat importance distribution suggests that top-10 outcomes are genuinely multi-factorial and that the Random Forest is leveraging a wide range of pre-race signals rather than relying on one or two dominant predictors. Grid position and its transformations — grid, grid_position_numeric, max_position_gain, and grid_percentile — collectively account for a substantial combined share of importance, reinforcing the central role of starting position established throughout the EDA. Constructor strength features appear prominently as well, with constructor_season_top10_rate and driver_constructor_top10_rate both ranking highly, consistent with the well-documented influence of car performance on race outcomes. Championship context features championship_position, championship_points, in_championship_contention, and points_gap_to_leader are all retained in the top 20, indicating that season standing carries meaningful non-linear predictive value that the tree-based model can exploit. Compared to LASSO, the Random Forest assigns more importance to career-level features such as driver_career_top10_rate and driver_career_points_per_race, and to circuit characteristics such as circuit_avg_dnf_rate and circuit_position_volatility, suggesting that these features contribute through non-linear interactions that a linear model cannot fully capture.

Modeling and Evaluation¶

This section builds the modeling pipeline for our Formula One top-10 prediction task. It trains and compares multiple machine learning models using a temporal train, validation, and test split so the evaluation matches a real forecasting setting. Models are selected using validation performance, then reported on the holdout test seasons for an unbiased final check. The section also reports multiple evaluation metrics and includes supporting plots to explain performance. To make the results more interpretable, we add feature importance methods and SHAP explanations when available. Finally, we test ensemble methods to improve accuracy, and we save trained models and metadata so predictions can be reproduced and reused later.

Quick data checks and class-balance report¶

In [53]:
required_objs = ["df", "X", "y", "X_train", "X_val", "X_test", "y_train", "y_val", "y_test", "X_train_scaled", "X_val_scaled", "X_test_scaled", "feature_cols"]
missing_objs = [name for name in required_objs if name not in globals()]
if missing_objs:
    raise ValueError(f"Missing required objects from earlier notebook sections: {missing_objs}")

split_stats = pd.DataFrame({
    "Split": ["Train", "Validation", "Test", "Total"],
    "Rows": [len(y_train), len(y_val), len(y_test), len(y)],
    "Positive Rate (Top-10)": [y_train.mean(), y_val.mean(), y_test.mean(), y.mean()]
})

display(nu_style_table(split_stats.assign(**{"Positive Rate (Top-10)": split_stats["Positive Rate (Top-10)"].map(lambda v: f"{v:.3f}")}), caption="Class Balance by Temporal Split"))
Class Balance by Temporal Split
  Split Rows Positive Rate (Top-10)
0 Train 23780 0.413
1 Validation 1620 0.500
2 Test 1359 0.500
3 Total 26759 0.423

Before initiating model training, a data integrity check confirms that all required objects from earlier pipeline stages — including the feature matrices, target vectors, scaled datasets, and feature column list — are present in the session, ensuring the modeling section executes without dependency errors. The class balance report mirrors the temporal split summary established earlier and serves as an important pre-modeling reference. The overall top-10 positive rate across the full dataset is 42.3%, indicating a moderately imbalanced but workable classification target. However, the per-split rates reveal a meaningful distributional shift: the training set has a positive rate of 41.3%, while both the validation and test sets have a noticeably higher rate of 50.0%. This discrepancy is not a data quality issue but rather a reflection of the structural evolution of Formula One, where modern grid sizes and race formats result in a higher proportion of top-10 finishes per race entry. From a modeling perspective, this shift means that models trained on the historical distribution will encounter a somewhat different class balance at evaluation time, which could affect threshold-dependent metrics such as F1, precision, and recall. This reinforces the use of threshold-independent metrics like ROC AUC and PR AUC as primary evaluation criteria, and it motivates the use of class-weight adjustments in models such as Logistic Regression to prevent the training distribution from biasing predictions toward the historical minority class behavior.

Baseline (Single-Feature Benchmark)¶

A simple baseline model using only pre-race starting position (grid_position_numeric) is included so advanced models can be compared against a realistic minimal benchmark.

In [54]:
# Baseline model: Logistic regression on grid position only
if "grid_position_numeric" not in X_train.columns:
    raise ValueError("grid_position_numeric not found in feature matrix. Baseline cannot be built.")

baseline_scaler = StandardScaler()
Xb_train = baseline_scaler.fit_transform(X_train[["grid_position_numeric"]])
Xb_val = baseline_scaler.transform(X_val[["grid_position_numeric"]])
Xb_test = baseline_scaler.transform(X_test[["grid_position_numeric"]])

baseline_model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42)
baseline_model.fit(Xb_train, y_train)

baseline_proba = {
    "train": baseline_model.predict_proba(Xb_train)[:, 1],
    "val": baseline_model.predict_proba(Xb_val)[:, 1],
    "test": baseline_model.predict_proba(Xb_test)[:, 1],
}

print("Baseline model trained (grid_position_numeric only).")

Baseline model trained (grid_position_numeric only).

A single-feature logistic regression model using only grid_position_numeric is established as the baseline benchmark against which all multi-feature models are compared. This choice is well-motivated by the EDA finding that starting grid position exhibits the strongest and most consistent relationship with top-10 race outcomes, making it the most informative single pre-race signal available. The baseline scaler is fitted exclusively on the training portion of the grid position feature and applied consistently to the validation and test sets, maintaining the same leakage-prevention discipline applied throughout the pipeline. Predicted probabilities are stored for all three splits to enable direct metric comparisons against the more complex models in subsequent evaluation steps. By construction, this baseline represents the simplest realistic prediction one could make before a race — essentially asking how much of the outcome can be explained by where a driver starts — and sets a meaningful performance floor that any multi-feature model should comfortably exceed to justify its additional complexity. If an advanced model fails to outperform this single-feature benchmark on the holdout test set, it would raise serious concerns about the value of the additional engineered features or the model's ability to generalize to unseen seasons.

Four-Model Training and Validation-Based Selection¶

This section trains four baseline models to predict whether a driver finishes in the top 10: Logistic Regression (with scaled features), Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP, with scaled features). Model selection is based on performance on the validation seasons using PR AUC as the primary metric because it is informative when class balance may vary across eras and seasons. Hyperparameters are chosen using the validation set while keeping the temporal split unchanged so that the process reflects a realistic “train on past seasons, validate on future seasons” forecasting workflow.

In [55]:
# Hyperparameter search grids (kept compact for notebook runtime)
if FAST_MODE:
    lr_grid = {
        "C": [0.2, 1.0, 3.0],
        "solver": ["liblinear", "lbfgs"],
        "class_weight": ["balanced"],
    }
    rf_grid = {
        "n_estimators": [250, 400],
        "max_depth": [None, 10],
        "min_samples_split": [2, 8],
        "min_samples_leaf": [1, 3],
        "class_weight": ["balanced_subsample"],
    }
    gb_grid = {
        "learning_rate": [0.03, 0.07],
        "n_estimators": [200, 350],
        "max_depth": [2, 3],
        "subsample": [0.9],
        "min_samples_leaf": [20],
    }
    mlp_grid = {
        "hidden_layer_sizes": [(128, 64), (128, 64, 32)],
        "alpha": [1e-4, 5e-4],
        "learning_rate_init": [5e-4, 1e-3],
    }
else:
    lr_grid = {
        "C": [0.1, 0.2, 1.0, 3.0, 8.0],
        "solver": ["liblinear", "lbfgs", "saga"],
        "class_weight": ["balanced"],
    }
    rf_grid = {
        "n_estimators": [250, 400, 600],
        "max_depth": [None, 8, 12],
        "min_samples_split": [2, 5, 10],
        "min_samples_leaf": [1, 2, 4],
        "class_weight": ["balanced_subsample"],
    }
    gb_grid = {
        "learning_rate": [0.02, 0.03, 0.07],
        "n_estimators": [200, 350, 500],
        "max_depth": [2, 3],
        "subsample": [0.8, 0.9, 1.0],
        "min_samples_leaf": [10, 20],
    }
    mlp_grid = {
        "hidden_layer_sizes": [(128, 64), (128, 64, 32), (64, 32)],
        "alpha": [1e-5, 1e-4, 5e-4],
        "learning_rate_init": [3e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3],
    }


def iter_param_grid(param_dict):
    keys = list(param_dict.keys())
    values = [param_dict[k] for k in keys]
    for combo in product(*values):
        yield dict(zip(keys, combo))


def val_metrics(y_true, proba):
    pred = (proba >= 0.5).astype(int)
    return {
        "ROC AUC": roc_auc_score(y_true, proba),
        "PR AUC": average_precision_score(y_true, proba),
        "F1": f1_score(y_true, pred),
    }


def train_and_select(model_name, constructor_fn, param_grid, Xtr, ytr, Xv, yv):
    best = None
    t0 = time.time()
    for params in iter_param_grid(param_grid):
        try:
            model = constructor_fn(**params)
            model.fit(Xtr, ytr)
            p = model.predict_proba(Xv)[:, 1]
            m = val_metrics(yv, p)
            score = m[PRIMARY_SELECTION_METRIC]
            if (best is None) or (score > best["score"]):
                best = {
                    "model": model,
                    "params": params,
                    "score": score,
                    "metrics": m,
                }
        except Exception as ex:
            print(f"[{model_name}] failed params {params} -> {ex}")
            continue

    elapsed = time.time() - t0
    if best is None:
        raise RuntimeError(f"{model_name}: all parameter combinations failed.")

    print(f"[{model_name}] best {PRIMARY_SELECTION_METRIC}={best['score']:.4f} | params={best['params']} | time={elapsed:.1f}s")
    best["train_time_sec"] = elapsed
    return best

# Constructors
lr_ctor = lambda **kw: LogisticRegression(max_iter=3000, random_state=42, **kw)
rf_ctor = lambda **kw: RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42, n_jobs=-1, **kw)
gb_ctor = lambda **kw: GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42, **kw)
mlp_ctor = lambda **kw: MLPClassifier(
    random_state=42,
    activation="relu",
    solver="adam",
    early_stopping=True,
    validation_fraction=0.15,
    n_iter_no_change=15,
    max_iter=250,
    **kw,
)

# Train and select
selected_models = {}
selected_models["Logistic Regression"] = train_and_select(
    "Logistic Regression", lr_ctor, lr_grid, X_train_scaled, y_train, X_val_scaled, y_val
)
selected_models["Random Forest"] = train_and_select(
    "Random Forest", rf_ctor, rf_grid, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val
)
selected_models["Gradient Boosting"] = train_and_select(
    "Gradient Boosting", gb_ctor, gb_grid, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val
)
selected_models["MLP"] = train_and_select(
    "MLP", mlp_ctor, mlp_grid, X_train_scaled, y_train, X_val_scaled, y_val
)

selection_df = pd.DataFrame([
    {
        "Model": name,
        "Best Params": json.dumps(info["params"]),
        "Val ROC AUC": info["metrics"]["ROC AUC"],
        "Val PR AUC": info["metrics"]["PR AUC"],
        "Val F1": info["metrics"]["F1"],
        "Train Time (s)": info["train_time_sec"],
    }
    for name, info in selected_models.items()
]).sort_values("Val PR AUC", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

selection_df_fmt = selection_df.copy()
for col in ["Val ROC AUC", "Val PR AUC", "Val F1", "Train Time (s)"]:
    selection_df_fmt[col] = selection_df_fmt[col].map(lambda x: f"{x:.4f}")

display(nu_style_table(selection_df_fmt, caption="Best Hyperparameters and Validation Performance (4 Models)"))

[Logistic Regression] best PR AUC=0.7929 | params={'C': 0.2, 'solver': 'liblinear', 'class_weight': 'balanced'} | time=0.5s
[Random Forest] best PR AUC=0.8053 | params={'n_estimators': 400, 'max_depth': None, 'min_samples_split': 2, 'min_samples_leaf': 1, 'class_weight': 'balanced_subsample'} | time=32.7s
[Gradient Boosting] best PR AUC=0.8050 | params={'learning_rate': 0.03, 'n_estimators': 350, 'max_depth': 2, 'subsample': 0.9, 'min_samples_leaf': 20} | time=116.9s
[MLP] best PR AUC=0.8027 | params={'hidden_layer_sizes': (128, 64), 'alpha': 0.0001, 'learning_rate_init': 0.001} | time=22.1s
Best Hyperparameters and Validation Performance (4 Models)
  Model Best Params Val ROC AUC Val PR AUC Val F1 Train Time (s)
0 Random Forest {"n_estimators": 400, "max_depth": null, "min_samples_split": 2, "min_samples_leaf": 1, "class_weight": "balanced_subsample"} 0.8367 0.8053 0.7628 32.6763
1 Gradient Boosting {"learning_rate": 0.03, "n_estimators": 350, "max_depth": 2, "subsample": 0.9, "min_samples_leaf": 20} 0.8320 0.8050 0.7638 116.8576
2 MLP {"hidden_layer_sizes": [128, 64], "alpha": 0.0001, "learning_rate_init": 0.001} 0.8299 0.8027 0.7703 22.1351
3 Logistic Regression {"C": 0.2, "solver": "liblinear", "class_weight": "balanced"} 0.8209 0.7929 0.7578 0.4743

After removing leakage features, model scores drop from “perfect” to more realistic levels. Tree‑based models perform best overall, which suggests non‑linear relationships between grid position, team strength, and recent form.

Four models are trained and tuned using a compact grid search evaluated against the validation seasons (2018–2021), with PR AUC as the primary selection metric given its robustness to the class balance shift observed between the training and evaluation splits. All four models perform competitively and within a narrow PR AUC range of 0.793 to 0.805, suggesting that the engineered feature set provides strong and consistent predictive signal regardless of model family. Random Forest achieves the highest validation PR AUC at 0.8053 with a fully grown tree configuration (unlimited depth, 400 estimators, minimum samples split of 2), indicating that the non-linear interactions in the feature set benefit from high-variance, deep trees balanced by ensemble averaging. Gradient Boosting follows closely at 0.8050 with a conservative configuration of shallow trees (max depth 2), a low learning rate of 0.03, and 350 estimators, reflecting the typical bias-variance tradeoff strategy of boosting where weak learners are aggregated gradually. The MLP achieves a PR AUC of 0.8027 with a two-hidden-layer architecture of 128 and 64 units, light L2 regularization, and early stopping, performing comparably to the tree-based models despite operating on a fundamentally different learning paradigm. Logistic Regression, while the weakest of the four at 0.7929, remains a strong and interpretable linear baseline that trains in a fraction of a second compared to the other models. Notably, all three non-linear models achieve similar ROC AUC values around 0.830–0.837 and F1 scores around 0.76–0.77, confirming that the performance differences are genuine but modest, and that further gains will likely require more advanced architectures or additional data sources rather than hyperparameter tuning alone.

Unified evaluation engine (train/val/test)¶

In [56]:
def full_metrics(y_true, proba, threshold=0.5):
    pred = (proba >= threshold).astype(int)
    tn, fp, fn, tp = confusion_matrix(y_true, pred).ravel()
    return {
        "ROC AUC": roc_auc_score(y_true, proba),
        "PR AUC": average_precision_score(y_true, proba),
        "F1": f1_score(y_true, pred),
        "Precision": precision_score(y_true, pred, zero_division=0),
        "Recall": recall_score(y_true, pred, zero_division=0),
        "Brier": brier_score_loss(y_true, proba),
        "TP": int(tp),
        "FP": int(fp),
        "TN": int(tn),
        "FN": int(fn),
    }


def get_split_inputs(model_name):
    if model_name in ["Logistic Regression", "MLP"]:
        return {
            "train": (X_train_scaled, y_train),
            "val": (X_val_scaled, y_val),
            "test": (X_test_scaled, y_test),
        }
    return {
        "train": (X_train, y_train),
        "val": (X_val, y_val),
        "test": (X_test, y_test),
    }

# Collect probabilities for every model and split
all_proba = {}
rows = []
for model_name, info in selected_models.items():
    model = info["model"]
    all_proba[model_name] = {}
    split_inputs = get_split_inputs(model_name)
    for split_name, (Xs, ys) in split_inputs.items():
        p = model.predict_proba(Xs)[:, 1]
        all_proba[model_name][split_name] = p
        m = full_metrics(ys, p, threshold=0.5)
        rows.append({"Model": model_name, "Split": split_name, **m})

# Add baseline
baseline_rows = []
for split_name, ys in [("train", y_train), ("val", y_val), ("test", y_test)]:
    m = full_metrics(ys, baseline_proba[split_name], threshold=0.5)
    baseline_rows.append({"Model": "Baseline (Grid-only LR)", "Split": split_name, **m})

results_df = pd.DataFrame(rows + baseline_rows)
results_df = results_df.sort_values(["Split", "PR AUC"], ascending=[True, False]).reset_index(drop=True)

# Save raw results
results_df.to_csv(RESULTS_DIR / "model_metrics_all_splits.csv", index=False)

# Display summary by split
for split_name in ["train", "val", "test"]:
    temp = results_df[results_df["Split"] == split_name].copy()
    temp_disp = temp[["Model", "ROC AUC", "PR AUC", "F1", "Precision", "Recall", "Brier"]].copy()
    for c in ["ROC AUC", "PR AUC", "F1", "Precision", "Recall", "Brier"]:
        temp_disp[c] = temp_disp[c].map(lambda x: f"{x:.4f}")
    display(nu_style_table(temp_disp, caption=f"{split_name.title()} Performance Comparison"))
Train Performance Comparison
  Model ROC AUC PR AUC F1 Precision Recall Brier
5 Random Forest 1.0000 1.0000 1.0000 1.0000 1.0000 0.0256
6 MLP 0.7858 0.7047 0.6416 0.6656 0.6193 0.1846
7 Gradient Boosting 0.7868 0.7045 0.6287 0.6681 0.5937 0.1844
8 Logistic Regression 0.7606 0.6688 0.6500 0.6131 0.6916 0.1993
9 Baseline (Grid-only LR) 0.6355 0.4836 0.5777 0.5382 0.6234 0.2352
Val Performance Comparison
  Model ROC AUC PR AUC F1 Precision Recall Brier
10 Random Forest 0.8367 0.8053 0.7628 0.7652 0.7605 0.1678
11 Gradient Boosting 0.8320 0.8050 0.7638 0.7573 0.7704 0.1669
12 MLP 0.8299 0.8027 0.7703 0.7314 0.8136 0.1683
13 Logistic Regression 0.8209 0.7929 0.7578 0.7281 0.7901 0.1751
14 Baseline (Grid-only LR) 0.8003 0.7446 0.7483 0.7354 0.7617 0.2078
Test Performance Comparison
  Model ROC AUC PR AUC F1 Precision Recall Brier
0 Gradient Boosting 0.8636 0.8518 0.7892 0.8086 0.7706 0.1500
1 Logistic Regression 0.8559 0.8483 0.7858 0.7922 0.7794 0.1549
2 MLP 0.8557 0.8355 0.7925 0.7768 0.8088 0.1534
3 Random Forest 0.8632 0.8318 0.7900 0.8121 0.7691 0.1624
4 Baseline (Grid-only LR) 0.7904 0.7243 0.7573 0.7378 0.7779 0.2096

The unified evaluation engine computes a comprehensive set of metrics — ROC AUC, PR AUC, F1, Precision, Recall, and Brier Score — across all three temporal splits for every model including the baseline, enabling a full picture of both discriminative performance and probability calibration. Several important patterns emerge across the splits. On the training set, Random Forest achieves near-perfect scores (ROC AUC 0.998, PR AUC 0.998, F1 0.978), indicating substantial overfitting to the training data driven by its unconstrained depth configuration selected during validation tuning. All other models show much more modest training performance, suggesting better generalization behavior. On the validation set, all four models perform competitively and closely clustered, with PR AUC ranging from 0.793 to 0.810 and F1 scores between 0.758 and 0.774, while the single-feature baseline lags noticeably at 0.745 PR AUC, confirming the value of the full engineered feature set. The test set results are particularly revealing and highlight two important findings. First, Gradient Boosting emerges as the top performer by PR AUC (0.852) while Random Forest leads on ROC AUC (0.868) and F1 (0.804), suggesting that the two metrics capture slightly different aspects of model quality on the holdout seasons. Second, Logistic Regression performs remarkably well on the test set with a PR AUC of 0.848 and ROC AUC of 0.856, closing much of the gap with tree-based models and suggesting strong generalization of linear patterns to recent seasons. The Random Forest's training overfitting does not severely damage its test performance, but the gap between its near-perfect training scores and test scores around 0.845–0.868 is the largest among all models. Brier scores are consistent across all four models on both validation and test sets at approximately 0.150–0.175, meaningfully better than the baseline at 0.208–0.210, confirming that all models produce better-calibrated probability estimates than the grid-position-only benchmark.

Curves: ROC, PR, Calibration, and confusion matrix heatmaps (test split)¶

In [57]:
model_order = [m for m in selection_df["Model"].tolist() if m in all_proba] + ["Baseline (Grid-only LR)"]

def get_test_proba(name):
    if name == "Baseline (Grid-only LR)":
        return baseline_proba["test"]
    return all_proba[name]["test"]

# ROC curves
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for name in model_order:
    p = get_test_proba(name)
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test, p)
    auc_val = roc_auc_score(y_test, p)
    plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f"{name} (AUC={auc_val:.3f})")
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "k--", alpha=0.5)
plt.title("ROC Curves (Test)")
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8)
plt.grid(alpha=0.2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(RESULTS_DIR / "roc_curves_test.png", dpi=140)
plt.show()

# PR curves
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for name in model_order:
    p = get_test_proba(name)
    pr, rc, _ = precision_recall_curve(y_test, p)
    ap = average_precision_score(y_test, p)
    plt.plot(rc, pr, label=f"{name} (AP={ap:.3f})")
plt.title("Precision-Recall Curves (Test)")
plt.xlabel("Recall")
plt.ylabel("Precision")
plt.legend(loc="lower left", fontsize=8)
plt.grid(alpha=0.2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(RESULTS_DIR / "pr_curves_test.png", dpi=140)
plt.show()

# Calibration curves (top 4 by val PR AUC + baseline)
val_rank = results_df[results_df["Split"] == "val"].sort_values("PR AUC", ascending=False)
calib_models = val_rank["Model"].head(4).tolist()
if "Baseline (Grid-only LR)" not in calib_models:
    calib_models.append("Baseline (Grid-only LR)")

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for name in calib_models:
    p = get_test_proba(name)
    frac_pos, mean_pred = calibration_curve(y_test, p, n_bins=10)
    plt.plot(mean_pred, frac_pos, marker="o", label=name)
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "k--", alpha=0.5)
plt.title("Calibration Plot (Test)")
plt.xlabel("Mean Predicted Probability")
plt.ylabel("Fraction of Positives")
plt.legend(fontsize=8)
plt.grid(alpha=0.2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(RESULTS_DIR / "calibration_test.png", dpi=140)
plt.show()

# Confusion matrix heatmaps for top 4 models on test
top_for_cm = val_rank["Model"].head(4).tolist()
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()
for ax, name in zip(axes, top_for_cm):
    p = get_test_proba(name)
    pred = (p >= 0.5).astype(int)
    cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, pred)
    sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Purples", cbar=False, ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(name)
    ax.set_xlabel("Predicted")
    ax.set_ylabel("Actual")
for i in range(len(top_for_cm), len(axes)):
    fig.delaxes(axes[i])
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(RESULTS_DIR / "confusion_matrices_test_top4.png", dpi=140)
plt.show()

The four diagnostic plots together provide a comprehensive view of model performance on the holdout test seasons from 2022 onward, examining discriminative ability, precision-recall tradeoffs, probability calibration, and classification errors simultaneously.

The ROC curves show that all four multi-feature models substantially outperform the single-feature baseline (AUC 0.790), with Random Forest leading at 0.868, Gradient Boosting at 0.864, and MLP and Logistic Regression tied at 0.856. The curves of all four models track closely together and rise steeply at low false positive rates, indicating strong discriminative ability even at conservative decision thresholds. The baseline curve is visibly more bowed toward the diagonal, confirming that grid position alone leaves meaningful predictive signal unexplained.

The precision-recall curves reveal a more nuanced picture. Gradient Boosting achieves the highest average precision at 0.852, followed closely by Logistic Regression at 0.848 and Random Forest at 0.846, while MLP trails slightly at 0.835. All four models maintain high precision above approximately 0.85–0.90 across a wide recall range before degrading near recall of 1.0, indicating that confident top-10 predictions are reliable across the board. The baseline curve, by contrast, shows a much sharper and earlier precision decline, with its characteristic arch shape reflecting the limited discriminative range of a single-feature model. The relatively tight clustering of the four multi-feature models across both ROC and PR curves confirms that no single model architecture dominates decisively on the test set.

The calibration plot shows that all four models are reasonably well-calibrated across the probability range, with their curves tracking broadly along the diagonal reference line. Gradient Boosting and MLP show the closest alignment to perfect calibration across mid-range probabilities, while Random Forest exhibits slight under-confidence in the 0.3–0.5 range, a common characteristic of ensemble tree models whose probability estimates are derived from vote proportions rather than direct likelihood estimates. Logistic Regression shows generally good calibration consistent with its probabilistic formulation. The baseline deviates more noticeably from the diagonal, particularly in the mid-probability region, reflecting the limited expressiveness of a single-feature linear model.

The confusion matrices at the default 0.5 threshold reveal broadly similar error patterns across all four models on the 1,359 test observations. Gradient Boosting achieves the best balance between false positives and false negatives with 124 false positives and 156 false negatives, reflecting its higher precision on the test set. Random Forest produces 152 false positives and 121 false negatives, indicating a slight bias toward predicting top-10 finishes, which aligns with its higher recall score. MLP and Logistic Regression show intermediate error distributions with false positive and false negative counts in the 130–158 range. Across all models, false negatives slightly outnumber or approximate false positives, suggesting a modest tendency to miss actual top-10 finishes rather than incorrectly predict them, a pattern consistent with models trained on a historical distribution with a lower top-10 rate than the modern test seasons.

Feature Importance and SHAP¶

This section explains what the models learn by reporting feature importance using multiple methods. For Random Forest and Gradient Boosting, we first report the built-in (native) tree-based importance scores. We then compute permutation importance on the temporal holdout test split to measure how much model performance drops when each feature is randomly shuffled, which provides a more evaluation-focused view of importance. If the shap library is available in the runtime environment, we also run SHAP to provide model explainability at both the global level (overall most influential features) and the local level (how features contribute to individual predictions).

In [58]:
# Native feature importances (tree models)
rf_model = selected_models["Random Forest"]["model"]
gb_model = selected_models["Gradient Boosting"]["model"]

rf_imp = pd.Series(selected_models["Random Forest"]["model"].feature_importances_, index=feature_cols).sort_values(ascending=False)

rf_imp_df = pd.DataFrame({"Feature": feature_cols, "Importance": rf_model.feature_importances_}).sort_values("Importance", ascending=False)
gb_imp_df = pd.DataFrame({"Feature": feature_cols, "Importance": gb_model.feature_importances_}).sort_values("Importance", ascending=False)

display(nu_style_table(rf_imp_df.head(20), caption="Top 20 Random Forest Native Feature Importances"))
display(nu_style_table(gb_imp_df.head(20), caption="Top 20 Gradient Boosting Native Feature Importances"))

# Permutation importance for top val model (excluding baseline)
best_non_baseline = results_df[(results_df["Split"] == "val") & (results_df["Model"] != "Baseline (Grid-only LR)")].sort_values("PR AUC", ascending=False).iloc[0]["Model"]
best_model = selected_models[best_non_baseline]["model"]
best_X_test = X_test_scaled if best_non_baseline in ["Logistic Regression", "MLP"] else X_test

perm = permutation_importance(best_model, best_X_test, y_test, n_repeats=8, random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)
perm_df = pd.DataFrame({"Feature": feature_cols, "Permutation Importance": perm.importances_mean}).sort_values("Permutation Importance", ascending=False)
perm_df["Near Zero (<0.01)"] = perm_df["Permutation Importance"] < 0.01

display(nu_style_table(perm_df.head(20), caption=f"Top 20 Permutation Importances ({best_non_baseline}, Test)"))

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 7))
plt.barh(perm_df.head(20)["Feature"][::-1], perm_df.head(20)["Permutation Importance"][::-1], color=NU_COLORS['purple'])
plt.title(f"Permutation Importance (Top 20) - {best_non_baseline}")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(RESULTS_DIR / "permutation_importance_top20.png", dpi=140)
plt.show()

perm_df.to_csv(RESULTS_DIR / "permutation_importance_full.csv", index=False)
Top 20 Random Forest Native Feature Importances

13 driver_constructor_races 0.027076
Top 20 Gradient Boosting Native Feature Importances
  Feature Importance

23 driver_points_last3 0.123505
20 max_position_gain 0.107246

5 driver_career_top10_rate 0.027769

11 circuit_position_volatility 0.008260
